headline: Geopolitical Fog Hangs Over the Open as Oil Volatility and Earnings Season Collide
body_html:
Wall Street is set to begin the week on edge after Friday's session delivered broad-based selling — 349 names in our 501-stock universe fell versus just 149 that advanced — as escalating U.S.-Iran hostilities kept energy markets unsettled and rattled investor confidence heading into the weekend. Treasury yields edged higher Friday, and the dollar drifted even as the conflict intensified, leaving few clear safe-haven signals for traders to lean on this morning.

Oil is the dominant macro variable at the open. U.S. pump prices crossed the four-dollar threshold again on renewed Middle Eastern fighting, and reports of tankers struck near the Strait of Hormuz have disrupted loading activity across the region. An Iranian signal toward fresh negotiations briefly reversed crude's advance late in the week, injecting a measure of two-way volatility that could persist through today's early hours. Energy was the sole sector to finish Friday in positive territory, gaining 0.77%, while Homebuilders bore the steepest losses at minus 3.05%.

Earnings season is the secondary catalyst to track. AI-linked hardware and software names face heightened scrutiny after Friday's sharp declines in Electronic Design Automation — a harbinger of how unforgiving the tape has become for any guidance disappointment. Watch the opening rotation carefully: if oil holds its gains and geopolitical headlines worsen, defensives and energy could again outperform a tech-heavy growth complex that is already showing cracks.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Disruptions Rattle Energy Supply Chains
briefs.body: Two oil tankers were reported struck and immobilised in the Strait of Hormuz following the ninth consecutive day of U.S. strikes on Iran. Tanker traffic entering the strait to load oil has dropped sharply, and floating LNG storage levels are rising — a sign that the supply crunch is already working its way through logistics networks.

briefs.headline: EDA Software Stocks Suffer Steep Friday Declines
briefs.body: Cadence Design and Synopsys fell 9.47% and 7.85%, respectively, on Friday, making Electronic Design Automation the hardest-hit pocket within an already-weak software sector that shed nearly 1% on the session. The paired selloff raises questions about order visibility in the semiconductor design pipeline as earnings season accelerates.

briefs.headline: Intuitive Surgical Leads All Decliners After Guidance Disappointment
briefs.body: The robotic-surgery leader tumbled 14.15% on Friday — the steepest single-session drop in our universe — dragging the broader Healthcare sector down 1.24%. The magnitude of the move underscores how little tolerance the current market has for any shortfall in forward guidance, particularly in premium-valued medical-device names.

briefs.headline: Ryanair Warns Fuel Spike Is Squeezing Airline Economics
briefs.body: Europe's largest low-cost carrier reported a profit decline of roughly one-third, attributing the shortfall to surging fuel costs tied to Middle East instability and softer fare pricing. The results serve as an early-cycle warning for the global travel industry: if oil remains elevated, margin pressure across airlines could broaden significantly this earnings season.

briefs.headline: Gold Steadies as Traders Weigh Escalation Against Fed Rate Path
briefs.body: Bullion held relatively firm Friday as investors balanced the safe-haven pull of intensifying U.S.-Iran hostilities against rising bets on further Federal Reserve rate increases. The metal's measured tone suggests markets are not yet pricing a full-scale regional war premium, though any deterioration in Hormuz shipping could shift that calculus quickly.

briefs.headline: Sonos and Seagate Buck the Tape With Standout Friday Gains
briefs.body: Against a broadly negative backdrop, Sonos surged 5.89% and Seagate Technology climbed 5.66% on Friday, topping our universe's leaderboard. The divergence is a reminder that stock-specific catalysts can override macro headwinds — a dynamic that strategists say argues for selectivity rather than wholesale risk reduction as earnings season unfolds.
